data.title: Ryanair: The Fuel Price Selloff Is Overdone, But Jet Fuel Shortages Are A Risk
data.description:
Amid rising oil prices and potential oil and fuel shortages, airlines in Europe are looking to cut costs. Ryanair Holdings plc (RYAAY) is no exception to that, and particularly for a low-cost carrier this may provide challenges. Since my last report, the company’s share prices have declined by 19%, clearly underperforming the S&P 500’s (SP500) 1.9% gain. In this report, I discuss Ryanair’s sensitivity to fuel prices, the partial shielding and risks.

Ryanair Has A Higher Proportional Share Of Fuel Costs

Ryanair is always looking to reduce costs, and that may leave Ryanair more exposed to fuel costs, because its fuel costs proportionally may make up a higher share of the operating expenses. In my prior report, I discussed the dispute between Elon Musk and Ryanair centering on the economic feasibility of installing satellite Internet on Ryanair’s fleet, and the company noted that there is a fuel penalty and in combination with the behavior of their cost-conscious customers, the installation of satellite Internet does not make financial sense for Ryanair. Satellite Internet installation requires an external antenna to be mounted on the aircraft, which brings a small fuel burn penalty. With the increase in fuel prices, that fuel burn penalty expressed in dollars is obviously going up. While the dispute may have seemed ridiculous to many, it is with this mindset and ROI scope that Ryanair has been able to run the business efficiently.

I ran the numbers for the prior three financial years, and what we note is that Ryanair’s fuel expenses account for 42%-45% of the operating expenses and 43% over the past three years. Ryanair is not a U.S. operator, but for reference purposes I have looked at the fuel cost exposure of U.S. carriers excluding other costs. That showed that fuel costs were 30% of all costs measured from 2022 until 2024. For mainline carriers, this was 31% and for ultra-low-cost carriers this was 39%. So, the impression that low-cost carriers proportionally have higher fuel costs is true.

The Shielding Mechanisms For Ryanair

The easy way for Ryanair to offset higher costs would be to increase air fares and I believe that will also happen. At the same time, the customers of low-cost carriers are cost-conscious meaning that Ryanair does not have a whole lot of space to increase prices.

That is where the element of shielding from higher cost on a cost level comes into play. There are two simple ways to cut fuel costs:

  1. Consume less fuel.
  2. Hedge against fuel price fluctuations.

Consuming less fuel can be done in two ways. The first one is simply by flying less. The obvious starting point would be to cut loss making routes. However, what also plays a role is that some airports may not be able to supply fuel or fuel in the quantities that Ryanair needs. So, that is also what Ryanair has to navigate. Currently, there is around 6 weeks of fuel left, so there is a big challenge for the summer season.

The other way to consume less fuel is by using fuel efficient airplanes. However, that is not going to help in the near-term in the sense that Ryanair cannot tell Boeing they need more fuel efficient now. In FY25, Ryanair reduced its fuel consumption per available seat-mile by around 1% and current fleet plans suggest similar reductions possible for FY26 and FY27. However, the jet fuel prices in Europe increased from around €2-€2.10 to €4.50 per gallon, which means that prices have more than doubled. A 1% reduction is a drop in the ocean.

Ryanair has around 84% of its Q4 consumption (quarter ending March) hedged at $77 per barrel. That means that 16% of its March consumption is exposed to the higher jet fuel prices. Effectively, that would mean a €2.14 per gallon cost. So, for the final quarter of Ryanair’s economic year there is an impact around four to fourteen eurocents per gallon against the pre-war pricing. That is 2%-7% that Ryanair pays above the pre-war market prices and shows the strength of hedging. At the same time, we note that against pre-war pricing, the expected economic price per gallon would have been €1.74-€1.76. So, for March the increase in the effective price per gallon would be around 22%.

For FY27, the blended price would be €2.08 per gallon which is still in the €2-€2.10 range. So, Ryanair’s hedging strategy leads them to have effective fuel prices that are more or less in line with the pre-war market prices, but the hedging benefit of €0.40-€0.52 per gallon against the pre-war prices is almost fully gone. Additionally, there are additional cost such as fuel handling costs or into-plane costs and carbon costs. So, the full effective impact is even bigger as the gallon prices

Ryanair Stock Remains A Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Ryanair, I am maintaining my Buy rating. It is clear that there is a risk, but that risk is not so much that fuel costs are going to explode since the company is largely shielded. However, fuel hedge gains are fading and that is why Ryanair may increase ticket prices and cut some flight activity. Overall, this could mean that the impact on Ryanair’s profits may not be major. However, it depends on how consumer sentiment and availability of jet fuel at European prices evolves. I believe the stock has 32% upside with a $75.77 price target.

If we look at margins, we see that those are holding up quite nicely. CapEx as a share of revenues will be in the 10%-15% range in the years ahead and roughly half of the capital operating cash flow will be used for capital expenditures with an increase in FY28 driven by the introduction of the Boeing 737 MAX 10. Ryanair is in a net cash position, which is a rather unique position for airlines to be in. By FY27, absent of any airplane delivery financing the company will have no bank debt on the balance sheet. This gives the company a lot of room to increase shareholder repurchase and particularly in FY27 there should be around $1.5 billion available to return to shareholders. That has currently not been modelled in when compiling the price targets.

Conclusion: Ryanair Should Be Able To Navigate Cost Impact

For Ryanair, the jet fuel crisis likely will result in fuel hedge gains substantially fading and a substantially higher than expected economic fuel cost per gallon even with 80% of the fuel consumption hedged for FY27. So, what Ryanair is likely doing when cutting costs and raising fares is attempting to offset the spread between new economic fuel cost per which is actually in line with pre-war prices, and the prior assumed economic fuel cost per gallon. For many airlines, higher fuel prices force higher air fares in order to remain profitable. For Ryanair, that is not so much the case I believe. It is more a margin preserving lever that they may pull.

I will be flying Ryanair in two weeks from now, and the main concern right now for me as a passenger, but also for shareholders is whether the airline has to cut flights due to the availability of jet fuel. So, there definitely are risks for Ryanair, but their fuel hedging strategy has proven to be a major de-risk.

data.title: Live Nation: Nothing Is Certain Yet, The Drama Continues
data.description:
Live Nation (LYV) was recently in the news because of a jury verdict that didn’t go its way, with possibilities of a breakup still possible. I wanted to go over the situation in more detail and give my opinion.

The latest on the lawsuit

On April 15th, a federal jury in New York found Live Nation and Ticketmaster illegally maintained monopoly power and overcharged consumers. This comes after a six-week trial with many witnesses, and according to the lawsuit, LYV overcharged customers for $1.72 per ticket over several years. There are also talks of the damages reaching around $700m if this amount is confirmed by the court. The judge has not set the final damages award, with LYV saying that the jury’s verdict is not the last word. It seems that the company has already accrued $280m toward state damages and civil penalties, so if $700m is approved, then it just needs to add another $420m on top.

That is a lot of dough. I am still quite surprised that the lawsuit went against Live Nation. I thought it would be thrown out, and what I am also surprised about is that they only overcharged customers by less than 2 bucks per ticket. Anytime I’m buying a ticket on Ticketmaster, I always feel robbed. It feels like the fees and everything else put the actual ticket price at least $10-$15 higher than it should have been. So, I guess I’m relieved that I’m not really overpaying in the grand scheme of things? I digress. Is the $700m penalty for Live Nation even meaningful enough that it would deter it from making the same mistake again? Let’s take a look at its financial performance and position.

As of FY25, the company’s revenues were $25.2B, up around 9% y/y, and operating income came in at $1.25B, up 51% y/y. So, a one-time charge of $700m would be a decent dent in the company’s performance, but not the end of the world. If we look at the company’s balance sheet, we can see that LYV has around $7B in cash, so that would be a 10% hit to its mountain of cash. It’s not nothing, but I doubt this is going to lead to a change in the company’s practices in the long run.

Ticketmaster Divestiture

To make a real impact on Live Nation, the judge has to look past petty penalties that never amount to any change in behavior. Companies with a boatload of cash will continue to break the rules if paying the penalties is cheaper than changing all of the operations. So, with the verdict of the jury, there is a good chance that Live Nation will have to divest its subsidiary Ticketmaster. So, let’s see how big an impact the divestiture of Ticketmaster is for Live Nation. Live Nation provides a good breakdown of its performance across its many subsidiaries, including Ticketmaster. For Q4, Ticketmaster’s revenues were up 3% to $3.1B. This puts Ticketmaster’s total revenue contributions at around 12%. That is a decent chunk as well, but not the end of the world. So, in theory, if Ticketmaster had to go, we would see a 12% dip in sales. It’s grand. However, if we dig into the profitability aspect, we will see that Ticketmaster is much more valuable to Live Nation than its $3B in sales. Ticketmaster runs at a much higher margin than its Concert revenue segment. LYV’s consolidated Adjusted Operating Income, or AOI, was around $2.36B. $1.134B of it came from Ticketmaster alone. That is around 48% of the total came from a segment that is responsible for only $3B in sales. Concert sales were close to $21B, and it made only $687m in AOI. So, a loss of Ticketmaster is actually going to hit the company’s operations quite a bit when we dig under the surface headline numbers. If that happens, then the company, essentially overnight, will be 48% less profitable.

How likely is the divestiture?

A lot of the reports suggest that the divestiture is not really on the table in modern antitrust cases. Even back when the DOJ was looking at an antitrust case back in January that ended without a resolution, a divestiture of Ticketmaster was highly unlikely. Furthermore, what helps the case of Ticketmaster staying in place right now under the Live Nation brand is that, as part of the DOJ settlement, LYV already has to pay $280m in settlements as well as to divest at least 13 amphitheater booking agreements. This could be enough for the judge, which could be seen as meaningful relief.

However, the jury’s liability also strengthens the states’ hand and gives them a lot more leverage to ask for bigger structural relief in the next phase. The breakup is still on the table, but I wouldn’t say it is the same as probable. I think the only way that the judge will order a breakup is if he honestly believes that this is the only way to restore competition.

Closing Comments

There is no way I am going anywhere near the company’s shares at this time. If there is even a small chance that the company will have to lose its cash cow, it will keep many investors away, including me. This would be a massive operational change, and a re-rating would be very likely to happen for Live Nation. It was already too expensive, so this re-rating is going to be to the downside.

In general, the verdict should be quite positive for the smaller players in this space, and I am looking forward to what that is going to mean for the consumer. I really don’t think we are going to see much lower prices for our concerts, but monopolies are never a good thing in my opinion, so I am hopeful, nonetheless.

data.title: Parnassus Growth Equity Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
The strategy pursues strong long-term returns by owning a concentrated portfolio of innovative, high-quality U.S. large cap companies that are well positioned to benefit from long-term secular trends.

Key Takeaways

  • Parnassus Growth Equity Fund (Investor Shares)(PFGEX) returned -9.63% (net of fees) during the quarter, slightly outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index's 1.12%.
  • Our holdings in the Information Technology, Industrials and Materials sectors boosted relative returns, while holdings in Health Care, Financials and Consumer Discretionary held back relative performance.
  • Amid market volatility due to artificial intelligence (AI) disruption fears and the war in Iran, we moved quickly to re-evaluate potential holdings, take advantage of price dislocations and ensure our overall portfolio positioning remained balanced.
  • Throughout any volatility, we will continue to execute our investment process and seek opportunities to invest in innovative, increasingly relevant and competitively advantaged businesses at attractive valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

Market Review

Growth stocks declined significantly in the first quarter, with the Russell 1000 Growth Index ending the quarter in negative territory. Market performance was impacted both by the beginning of a joint military offensive against Iran at the end of February as well as shifting narratives—and increasing concerns—about the potential for AI disruption, particularly in software. Performance varied across sectors, with Energy far outperforming other market segments as the conflict in Iran constrained global supply, pushing oil prices higher. The Financials, Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary sectors posted double-digit losses amid inflationary pressures and negative investor sentiment around the impact of AI. Strength related to AI infrastructure, particularly among manufacturers of semiconductors and related equipment, as well as companies benefiting from electrification, was outweighed by weakness in software, where concerns around AI-driven disruption led to widespread valuation compression.

Quarterly Performance Review

Positioning in Information Technology boosted relative results

The Fund (Investor Shares) returned -9.63% (net of fees) during the quarter, slightly outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index's -9.78%. Our holdings in the Information Technology, Industrials and Materials sectors aided relative returns, while holdings in Health Care, Financials and Consumer Discretionary curbed relative performance.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Applied Materials (AMAT), which supplies semiconductor manufacturing equipment, benefited from sustained demand tied to AI infrastructure build-out, as hyperscalers increased capital spending to support accelerating compute needs.

ASML Holding (ASML), a supplier of equipment for producing advanced microchips, gained on the strength of demand for advanced lithography, reinforcing its essential role in next-generation AI chip production.

GE Vernova (GEV), a leading global energy equipment manufacturer, benefited from surging electricity needs tied to data center build-out amid the continuing growth in AI-related infrastructure.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.'s (TSMC)(TSM) central role in AI chip production continued to bolster investor confidence and drive stock gains, as company management announced higher-than-anticipated capital expenditure guidance.

Linde (LIN), a multinational provider of industrial gases, gained as investors favored companies with durable, hard-to-disrupt business models tied to both the global economy and semiconductor manufacturing.

Bottom Quarterly Contributors

Boston Scientific (BSX), a medical device company, declined on concerns about increased competition and slowing market growth for its atrial fibrillation product, Farapulse, and mixed reaction to overall positive clinical data, though we believe its long-term growth prospects remain intact.

AppFolio (APPF), a provider of cloud-based software for property management, declined amid a broad sell-off within software driven by fears that AI could disrupt traditional software-as-a-service business models.

Atlassian (TEAM) fell along with its software peers as investors believed its emphasis on developer-focused tools presents a risk of AI disruption. We exited the position during the quarter, due to AI disruption concerns and its high valuation.

Ares Management (ARES), an alternative asset manager with a large franchise in private credit, underperformed due to retail redemptions, worries about loans to software private equity buyouts and rising default risk in direct lending.

Salesforce (CRM) stock fell sharply as investor sentiment on software companies broadly weakened amid AI disruption concerns, despite the company's favorable sales figures and margin growth. We view Salesforce as well positioned to capture AI upside given its specialized products and integrated products trading at an attractive valuation.

Portfolio Positioning

Maintaining balance while responding to volatility and shifting AI dynamics

We continue to maintain a portfolio positioning balanced between offense and defense. During the quarter, we looked for companies capable of resilient growth in multiple market opportunities, including those with substantial addressable markets or exceptionally strong secular drivers. In the current environment, with the conflict in Iran spiking oil prices and exacerbating market volatility, we believe companies with these characteristics can durably maintain growth.

Amid market volatility, we moved quickly to re-evaluate current and potential holdings and take advantage of dislocations. We continue to be disciplined in our evaluation of new investments, while remaining nimble and ready to move as opportunities arise. We made several position changes during the quarter, closing six positions and adding six new ones, while adjusting exposures to reflect the changing environment. The portfolio's largest overweights are in Health Care and Materials, while its largest underweights are in Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary.

Within Information Technology, we repositioned our software exposure, as AI disruption concerns drove a broad sell-off. We exited or trimmed lower-conviction software names to reduce our overall exposure and added new positions in Shopify and Oracle. In Communication Services, we initiated a position in Meta Platforms following share price weakness tied to increased investment spending. We shifted to a slightly more defensive positioning in Health Care, reducing cyclical exposure by selling Thermo Fisher Scientific and adding Medline, a healthcare distributor with a large market share gain opportunity, and Edwards Lifesciences, an innovative medical device company with a strong product path over the next three years.

We continue to seek opportunities to invest in innovative, increasingly relevant, competitively advantaged businesses at attractive valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

Outlook

Renewed optimism amid volatility and AI-driven change

While the first quarter saw a shift in the geopolitical backdrop, with escalating tensions and rising energy prices, our constructive view on growth equities remains unchanged as the macro and company backdrop continues to be relatively strong.

U.S. companies have proven they can navigate extraordinary challenges—from the pandemic to inflation to geopolitical conflicts—while continuing to innovate and grow earnings. The AI investment cycle and adoption continue to accelerate as well. Along with the increasing adoption of agentic AI, technology companies have been deploying enormous capital to build the infrastructure required for the next generation of computing. This investment wave remains one of the most important structural forces shaping the global economy. At the same time, the recent software sell-off reflects legitimate concerns around disruption, though we believe outcomes will vary across sectors and companies, reinforcing the value of active management.

The U.S. economy has also remained steady, even as gross domestic product growth and the labor market have been slowing recently. As the war in Iran drives energy prices higher, we are monitoring potential resurgence of inflation and its economic impacts should the conflict be prolonged. While higher energy prices can temporarily tighten liquidity, we expect global liquidity to ease and remain a tailwind for the stock market in the long run.

Geopolitical uncertainty is capping valuation multiples, creating greater dispersion across sectors and companies. With less room for multiple expansion, companies must deliver durable cash flow and real productivity gains. Given that growth equities can be volatile, with higher risk and higher reward, we continue to emphasize downside protection and disciplined investing. We view periods like the current environment, marked by volatility and geopolitical tension, as sources of opportunity and believe that many of our stocks can see multiples rerate as they prove the durability of growth.

Against this backdrop, we remain focused on consistent execution of our investment process, using periods of volatility to selectively invest in innovative, increasingly relevant, and competitively advantaged businesses at attractive valuations. By maintaining discipline through uncertainty, we aim to position the portfolio for strong returns over time.

Quarterly Portfolio Activity

Activity Security Name Ticker Sector Rationale
Bought Meta Platforms, Inc., Class A META Communication Services Meta has a strong competitive position and long-term relevance in digital advertising. Its family of apps reaches billions of people and offers powerful targeting tools, making it a key partner for advertisers as ad spending and AI-driven marketing continue to move online. We also value Meta's track record of innovation. We are actively monitoring its social and data privacy risks and believe the company has made progress on strengthening its policies and enforcement.
Bought Edwards Lifesciences Corp. EW Health Care Edwards Lifesciences develops innovative cardiac devices that reduce patient recovery times and enhance patient outcomes. We believe the market is underestimating the durability of Edwards' transcatheter aortic valve replacement franchise and the opportunity for its transcatheter mitral and tricuspid therapies.
Bought Shopify, Inc., Class A SHOP Information Technology Shopify has evolved from a small to midsize business tool into a unified commerce platform, increasingly winning larger, more complex customers while continuing to gain share internationally. Its integrated ecosystem across storefronts, payments, point of sale and merchant services creates strong network effects and high switching costs. Shopify is also well positioned to benefit from the next phase of e-commerce, including AI-enabled and agent-driven shopping experiences, supported by disciplined execution and existing large language model partnerships.
Bought Medline, Inc., Class A MDLN Health Care As the leading U.S. distributor of medical-surgical products and supply chain solutions, Medline's scale drives lower costs, high service levels and strong customer retention. Medline's increasing penetration of its private label products also enhances its growth and margins. With a long track record of share gains through new contracts and proprietary products, Medline can deliver steady returns, in our view.
Bought Oracle Corp. ORCL Information Technology Oracle is at an inflection point in its evolution from a mature software provider to a faster-growing cloud and AI platform with durable competitive advantages. Adoption of Oracle's next-generation cloud infrastructure for AI training workloads is driving a reacceleration in revenue and earnings growth, while its mission-critical database and enterprise franchises provide downside mitigation. With valuation still reflecting lingering skepticism around this transformation, we believe Oracle offers an attractive risk/reward profile as earnings compound.
Bought Viking Holdings Ltd. VIK Consumer Discretionary Viking offers a resilient growth profile, with a leading share in luxury river cruising and a loyal, affluent customer base that tends to book early and repeat at high rates. Supported by favorable travel demand trends, exclusive port access and a standardized fleet that drives operating efficiency, Viking has a business model that is well positioned to deliver durable growth with less cyclicality than traditional cruise peers.
Sold Workday, Inc., Class A WDAY Information Technology We exited our position in Workday to bring the portfolio's software exposure closer to market weight. Despite rising uncertainty in software, we continue to see select opportunities, maintaining positions in companies with minimal AI risk and those already derated but showing signs of near-term improvement.
Sold Datadog, Inc., Class A DDOG Information Technology We exited our position in Datadog to bring the portfolio's software exposure closer to market weight. Despite rising uncertainty in software, we continue to see select opportunities, maintaining positions in companies with minimal AI risk and those already derated but showing signs of near-term improvement.
Sold Insulet Corp. PODD Health Care While we continue to appreciate Insulet's long-term opportunity in Type 2 diabetes, we grew concerned that elevated expectations, increasing competition and a premium valuation could lead to greater volatility. We reallocated capital to Edwards Lifesciences, as we believe its product catalysts and valuation offer a more attractive risk/reward.

Quarterly Portfolio Activity (Continued)

Activity Security Name Ticker Sector Rationale
Sold Atlassian Corp., Class A TEAM Information Technology Atlassian has become increasingly driven by AI-related debate rather than underlying fundamentals, leading to higher volatility and a wider range of outcomes. We felt the risk/reward opportunity had become less compelling and redeployed the capital into Shopify, where we see strong competitive advantages and a clearer path to long-term value creation.
Sold Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc. TMO Health Care While Thermo Fisher remains a high-quality franchise, we saw better relative upside in names with less macro sensitivity and a greater share gain opportunity and reallocated the capital to Medline.
Sold Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. CMG Consumer Discretionary We exited our Chipotle position due to concerns around ongoing restaurant and consumer weakness, as well as demand headwinds from GLP-1 adoption, particularly following the oral launch. With these pressures weighing on near-term fundamentals, we chose to reallocate capital toward Viking, which we believe offers a more resilient growth profile.

data.title: The War Premium Is Being Sold: What The Tape Is Telling Us
data.description:
Since the Iran war began, the market has behaved like it usually does in a real-time stress test: it paid up for protection first, then began to withdraw that payment once the worst-case path didn’t continuously intensify. The clearest message is that the “war hedge” complex (oil, energy equities, even gold) has stopped acting like the only game in town, while broad equities, particularly tech/growth, have resumed leadership. That doesn’t mean the war is irrelevant. It means the marginal buyer is no longer willing to pay an ever-rising premium for it.

My approach here is simple: don't argue the headlines. Watch what stops working. When the hedge stops working, the next money is often made in the reversal, owning what the hedge was suppressing, until the tape tells you the war premium is back.

1) The Setup: Shock hedges worked, then they didn’t

In the rearview, the market clearly treated the conflict as a real constraint. Oil and energy are still massively positive on the longer windows: crude is up big over 6M and YTD, and energy equities have held strong returns as well. That’s the signature of a market that initially priced disruption risk into the commodity complex and into the sectors most levered to it.

But the more important information is what’s happening now. Over the last month and week, crude is down, and XLE has softened on a 1M basis even though it remains strong over 6M/YTD. At the same time, risk is reappearing in places it typically doesn’t if the market believes it’s entering a durable oil shock regime: QQQ and XLK are strong over 1M, SPY is firm, and even higher-beta expressions (ARKK) and cyclically sensitive segments (KRE) show meaningful 1M strength.

That combination, “crisis winners rolling over short-term while broad risk strengthens,” is the market’s way of saying: the shock was real, but the premium is being monatized.

2) The Lie Detector: Crude's reaction function

In geopolitical episodes, crude is the closest thing you get to a lie detector because it sits at the intersection of psychology and real-world constraints. The key question is not whether oil is high or low. It's whether it can continue to rise in response to bad news.

Your snapshot implies that, at least over the last month, crude has struggled to sustain the bid. That matters because crisis pricing has a specific feel: oil grinds higher on any hint of escalation, volatility stays sticky, and equities either struggle or narrow. When that pattern breaks, when oil stops rising despite the story still being “serious,” it often signals that the market has moved from panic pricing to probability weighting.

This is my main point: the edge isn't predicting the event; it’s recognizing when the market’s reflex has changed. The reflex shift is what creates reversals.

3) Why this is an opportunity set, not a prediction

We didn’t need to forecast peace or escalation to make money. We needed to know what the market was already discounting and when leadership was rotating. Right now, the market appears to be discounting a world in which the conflict remains a risk, but not one that continuously worsens into a sustained supply shock. That is why the war hedge complex can be strong on long lookbacks yet weak on the tactical windows, while equities broaden.

In other words, the opportunity is not war ends. The opportunity is the premium stops expanding. Those are very different trades. One is narrative. The other is tape.

4) The Playbook: Where the asymmetric trades tend to live

If the market is actively unpricing the war premium, the cleanest tactical expression is to fade the hedge and own the beneficiaries of easing fear. In practice, that often looks like rotating away from energy beta and into growth leadership, sometimes explicitly via relative trades (energy vs. tech), sometimes implicitly by trimming the hedge exposure and redeploying into the leaders.

The discipline is conditional. You don’t press that view because you feel clever; you press it because the market is telling you that the hedge is no longer being reinforced. The confirmation comes from relative strength: if XLE continues to lag SPY/QQQ and crude fails to reclaim momentum, the unwind can persist longer than feels comfortable. That's typically how these reversals pay. Keep an eye on software stocks that were destroyed during the Iran affairs.

At the same time, it’s important not to confuse “oil fade” with “capex fade.” Even if crude mean-reverts, the second-order consequences of conflict, security priorities, energy independence, infrastructure build-out can remain structurally supportive for certain pockets of the market. This is where a Druck-style framework becomes powerful: separate the headline trade (war hedge) from the structural trade (persistent investment cycle). It’s entirely possible for oil to cool while the “picks and shovels” of the buildout remain bid, because the world doesn’t unlearn the lesson of vulnerability overnight.

That’s the subtle opportunity: fade the crowded hedge while keeping exposure to the longer-duration beneficiaries of the new constraint set.

5) What would change the thesis quickly

This framework is only as good as its invalidation rules. The fastest way to be wrong is for crude to reassert itself, not with a one-day spike but with a decisive breakout that holds and re-drives energy leadership. If oil starts making higher highs and sustaining them, and if energy equities reclaim leadership versus the broader market, the tape is telling you the war has returned as a binding macro constraint. In that world, the reversal trade ends, and you rotate back toward protection.

On the other hand, if crude remains heavy despite continued headline noise, and equities keep broadening, with cyclicals and other higher-beta segments participating, the market is effectively confirming that the premium is being sold and that risk is being repriced higher.

The key is to treat crude behavior as the signal, not your interpretation of the news.

6) Portfolio posture: A two-speed stance

The most sensible posture in this kind of tape is a barbell that respects both realities. Tactically, you lean into the leadership rotation as long as the war hedge continues to fail: growth and quality risk can work if the market keeps un-pricing tail fears. Structurally, you maintain exposure to the capex and security implications that don't vanish just because the front-month premium comes off. That’s how you avoid being right about oil and wrong about the world.

You are not trying to be an oracle. You are trying to be positioned where the market’s odds are improving, and you are trying to exit quickly when the market tells you those odds have changed.

Closing thought

The most important pattern since February 28 isn’t that war caused X. It’s that the market absorbed the shock, paid aggressively for hedges, and is now selectively withdrawing that payment while re-risking into leadership sectors. That shift from reinforcement to failure is where reversals and opportunity typically emerge.
