data.title: TSMC: Foundry Monopoly Is Cracking As Rivals Catch Up (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
TSMC: Capacity Shortages Now A Big Problem

Investors of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) were likely befuddled as the leading edge foundry saw its stock struggle for momentum post-earnings.

After all, I hardly find anything in TSMC’s first-quarter earnings scorecard that could lead me to reach out to my blood pressure machine.

It was business as usual. Another quarter, and another solid earnings release.

It's almost like clockwork right now for TSM management, ringing the cash register and benefiting from the unprecedented AI infrastructure buildout.

And, really, what’s there to nitpick about these numbers?

And that’s despite N3 still ramping up, as it accounted for 25% of wafer revenue.

Given how crucial the N2 & N3 nodes are for AI HPC through at least the end of 2027, I believe TSMC will continue to retain and even improve margins further as the most leading-edge footprint grows.

However, given how quickly semiconductor stocks have recovered from the April bottom, I think it's now more difficult for me to simply ignore the observation that the market went into this Q1 print with even higher optimism.

And the possibility that TSM has been facing a quandary over increasing even more N3 capacity has been well documented.

We already know that the supply constraints in the TSMC foundry have already resulted in gains for Samsung Foundry (SSNLF) with Tesla (TSLA).

In addition, Intel has also gotten a new lease of life with Elon Musk’s SpaceX (SPACE), as the soon-to-be first trillionaire of the world wants to get his hands on 1 TW of capacity by building in his fabs.

By now, we know they are called Terafab, and it appears that Intel has gotten the nod from Musk in this potentially massive partnership. But, the execution is still a big question mark, by the way.

And some could argue that while Musk may not be wrong, he is often too early in his predictions.

So, a large dose of skepticism on these fabs being ready by the end of 2027 isn't unreasonable, so to speak.

Back in March, I had kept my Buy rating on TSM despite emerging threats from these competitors.

I think it’s getting clearer that the constraints in foundry capacity have impelled customers to look elsewhere, especially since TSM will most certainly need several years to ramp up any new capacity growth.

TSMC: Valuation Bifurcation Versus Memory Peers

Besides that, I guess chipmaking investors have also turned their eyes to their memory peers.

Let's be 100% honest about it. It is not as if the market has not baked super high optimism into their performance, right?

With TSM having delivered >140% in total return over the past one year, I don't have much to complain about being a shareholder.

That said, it’s also possible that the market is telling us that if the AI infrastructure buildout is making erstwhile memory stocks less cyclical than they used to be historically, then there could be a lot more upside to the memory plays.

Especially, since they are still priced at a relative discount to TSM, right?

To be forthright about it, I'm actually not very sure whether we should ignore the past painful history of memory stocks. Yet, it does seem like this valuation bifurcation between TSM and its memory peers has widened, while TSM has underperformed.

So, I guess HBM chip constraints have become an even more pertinent opportunity that semiconductor investors are trying to go into, believing that the logic side of it, which TSMC specializes in, is less compelling, relatively speaking.

So, this tug of war between logic and memory could also be pushing some investors away toward the memory side of the house.

Also, I guess there are several principal considerations that could have troubled these investors.

Number 1. TSM is now expecting the CapEx for 2026 to print closer to the top end of its guidance range of between $52 billion and $56 billion.

TSMC Capacity Ramp Up Takes Time

However, we also know that the capacity may not come online so soon.

Management had already emphasized that it takes quite a bit of time to build and ramp capacity.

These are multi-billion dollar facilities, right? They are highly complex, and require significant domain expertise.

Plus, validation truly takes time, and then you need to make sure your yields are good enough.

In any case, getting to volume production could reasonably take about four to five years, give or take.

I guess that's where the problem now is. Because of how quickly the AI demand has evolved.

Will the hyperscalers wait for TSM’s capacity to be ready?

I guess if you read about Intel’s partnership with Elon Musk, and management also getting more optimistic about snagging orders for advanced packaging, then Taiwan Semi might be really facing significant pressure for not having invested in even more capacity in the first place.

Number 2. We all know Nvidia (NVDA) is now banking on the success of Groq to help it maintain the clout over the AI ecosystem.

Rivals see the opportunity to regain their own respective narrative as CPUs become more critical to AI agentic inference prospects.

But the problem with the LPU was made by Samsung, right? TSMC management also didn’t deny this, although they indicated that they are working with their "customer (Nvidia)" closely on their next-gen LPU.

Therefore, this makes sense as to why the market could be seeing some cracks in the thesis right now.

It could indicate that archrivals such as Samsung and Intel are close to making crucial inroads into TSM’s stronghold.

They don't need to take the lion's share, and it's TSMC's majority share to lose.

Will this really crater TSMC’s market share right now? Not likely, right?

Could TSMC's Margins Have Peaked?

After all, TSM’s CapEx is still expected to increase through 2028, although the level of investment growth should decelerate. Meaning, operating leverage growth could slow?

But, let's face it, yeah?

CapEx intensity is always a concern for any chipmaker. You overinvest when the cycle turns, the market will punish you, as your P&L weakens at the wrong moment.

You underinvest when the cycle hasn't turned, you face challenges in having enough capacity, and you lose share to rivals.

And we know ramping up a foundry takes time. It's not that simple and straightforward. Very tough business to be in.

As for TSMC, the CapEx intensity is still generally aligned with past cycles.

For FY2027 and FY2028, the CapEx to revenue ratio is still projected to print 30.4% and 28% based on the latest estimates.

So, Wall Street hasn't gone bonkers thinking that the foundry will go into irrational exuberant mode after all, right?

Therefore, I think the bullish case on TSM is still quite well balanced at the right valuations and buy points.

To be clear, TSM is definitely operating well ahead of its long-term gross margin outlook of "56% and higher" here.

So, margin normalization risks aren’t immaterial, even though they may not be imminent. But, it’s still a thing worth watching.

And then, when we take a closer look at TSM’s valuation profile, it does appear that the market has been somewhat reluctant to take it higher past 22.5x on forward earnings since October 2024.

Plus, while TSM’s more ambitious CapEx guidance clearly points to strong and robust AI demand in the next three to five years, the threat of seeing peak margins might also affect the market’s sentiment here.

Buy Or Sell TSM Stock?

Taking into account all these challenges, I’m beginning to see TSM as having likely peaked in the medium term at below the $390 price level.

It’s obvious from TSM’s chart that buyers haven’t been able to clear that level since early this year.

Sure, it's not considered a bull trap, so I wouldn't suggest that this appears to be a long-term peak.

But the price action is demonstrating clear signs of weakness, despite having posted another remarkable release.

That to me indicates that investors are starting to ask more introspective questions about whether TSM's CapEx ramp up could be a little too late.

For me, I will think that the $320 level offers a clear indication of buying support from dip buyers. So, with the print now not looking favorable, I wouldn't be surprised if that level gets retested again subsequently.

For now, it's time to be patient and wait.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pinterest: Monetization Gap Alone Justifies Upside
data.description:
Pinterest (PINS) occupies a unique position as a high-intent, visually driven discovery platform and a differentiated digital advertiser. A key difference between Pinterest and other social media platforms is that instead of relying on passive content consumption, users are searching with intent. Around 90% of users visit the site for purchase planning, and ~50% of all searches have commercial intent. This direct relationship between user engagement and consumer purchasing behavior allows for long-term demand generation for advertising on the platform.

International monetization is a primary source of future upside. While the US will continue to have the highest ARPU globally for Pinterest, there is evidence that monetization efforts abroad are gaining traction. Accelerating European ARPU growth and rising international revenue signal that the monetization playbook is effective. If even partially converged toward US levels, this would create several hundred million dollars of incremental revenue without requiring any increase in user base. This creates upside through internal monetization alone.

In addition to AI improving the user experience and advertiser experience, it also improves the overall commerce experience. Models such as OmniSage and PinFM drive higher save rates among users, while models such as Performance+ drive materially higher conversion rates among advertisers. Furthermore, internal AI development reduces reliance on third-party models, which enables margin expansion opportunities as usage increases.

With the recent announcement of the $3.5 billion buyback program initiated by Elliott Investment Management, capital allocation could become an additional catalyst. In addition, with over $1.25 billion in FCF and very high conversion of EBITDA to FCF, Pinterest has plenty of resources to pursue this initiative while continuing to invest in organic growth initiatives.

Finally, with the acquisition of tvScientific and the entry into connected TV, Pinterest now has the opportunity to monetize high-intent data outside of their app. Although this introduces some near-term margin pressure, the long-term upside appears worth it. Given that Pinterest trades at ~12x forward earnings, it seems investors are undervaluing this and other catalysts. Accordingly, I rate the stock a Buy.

What Pinterest Has Built

Pinterest is the only big player in the world of high-intent, large-scale visual search and discovery. Social media feeds are made to passively consume content, whereas Pinterest users often arrive with commercial intent. This typically involves users coming to the site with a clear idea or need (a kitchen, a wedding, a fitness program, etc.). According to Pinterest, it has 80+ billion monthly searches and 1.7+ billion monthly click-throughs, which send users directly to retailers. There is a direct and tangible relationship between Pinterest and purchasing habits, which separates it from the majority of other social media sites.

The demographics of the users also add some urgency to the situation. As of today, Gen Z makes up over 50% of Pinterest's user base, growing faster than any other group of users on the platform. Gen Z is the most visually savvy consumer generation to date, making them more likely to find new brands via Pinterest instead of through paid advertising. Additionally, the generation still hasn't reached their full buying potential. This makes the consumer group that Pinterest is developing relationships with today the most important for long-term growth.

The Monetization Gap

The most interesting statistic for Pinterest is the ARPU across regions. For example, Pinterest UCAN ARPU was $9.41 in Q4 2025, an increase of 4% YoY. However, this ARPU varies widely throughout other geographies where the company made just $1.59 in Europe and $0.27 in the rest of the world. Therefore, those 514 million international users generate less than $1 in ARPU on average, while a UCAN user makes $9.41. Any closing of this gap, which I see as likely, could result in strong incremental revenue growth.

There is already data to support this closing, with the Europe ARPU increasing 15% YoY during Q4 2025 and 31% during Q3 2025. Moreover, international revenue increased by 38% YoY in 2025. Each of the factors necessary to execute the U.S.-based model has been put into place, with shopping ad formats, Performance+ products, and the lower-funnel infrastructure, which has been developed through significant investment over the past few years. With a gap this wide, any moderate international execution could produce meaningful revenue growth without help from the US division.

AI is Working on Both Sides of the Platform

Pinterest's investments in AI have had a real impact on both user interaction and advertising performance. For users, Pinterest has developed a proprietary AI model called OmniSage, which was trained on the company's visual and behavioral data. This model has driven a 450 bps improvement in total sitewide savings. Additionally, PinFM provides users with real-time recommendations based on a summary of the user's past actions, creating an additional 240 bps improvement in saves. These saves lead to increased levels of engagement, greater frequency of visitation, and therefore more commercial searches. Navigator-1 is Pinterest's proprietary AI architecture and has reduced costs associated with the use of third-party AI solutions by ~90%. With this closed-loop system, they can improve margins and show an advertiser exactly what they received for their ad spend.

For advertisers, Pinterest Performance+, an AI-enhanced advertising solution, is showing real results. In tests of this solution, advertisers experienced an average 64% increase in conversions of new customers when compared to control groups. ROAS bidding is currently available, and New Customer Acquisition Beta is in development. The SMB business, which accounts for 15% of Pinterest's revenue, doubled its growth rate in 2025. Pinterest Assistant, an AI-powered visual search engine, was recently released, and the initial results indicate a 25% increase in commercial intent when using the engine. These metrics represent more than incremental gains; they represent the foundation of a closed-loop commerce platform, enabling Pinterest to demonstrate to advertisers specifically what they received for their ad spend. Besides visibility, it also raises margins by developing and deploying internally rather than relying on proprietary AI.

Elliott and a $3.5 Billion Buyback

On March 3, 2026, Elliott purchased a $1 billion stake in Pinterest. Earlier that same month, Pinterest authorized a $3.5 billion stock buyback plan, which also allows for up to $2 billion in near-term repurchases. Given that the current market capitalization of Pinterest is ~$11.5 billion, the purchase of $2 billion worth of shares equates to nearly 17% of shares outstanding.

Additionally, Pinterest generated over $1.25 billion in FCF in 2025, which represented a 33% YoY increase and a 99% conversion from adjusted EBITDA. This ultimately gives Pinterest sufficient financial resources to implement its buyback plan without causing any undue strain on its balance sheet. I see this as a vote of confidence behind Pinterest's AI-driven momentum, which could help close the geographical gap and increase multiples.

tvScientific

One of Pinterest's more underappreciated decisions was its announcement in December 2025 of an agreement to acquire tvScientific, a connected TV performance advertising platform. The acquisition is expected to be completed in the first half of 2026. The reasoning behind this move makes good sense; Pinterest has one of the highest levels of commercial intent among all major platforms. However, it has traditionally only been able to monetize this intent while users were logged into the app.

This new acquisition enables Pinterest to expand upon its intent signals to include those streaming media through connected TVs. As a result, users can be targeted on their viewing habits based on their activity on Pinterest, linking Pinterest activity to TV viewing behavior. In terms of performance-based advertising, tvScientific offers a true behavioral data layer, rather than simply targeting demographics. For retail advertisers, who represent the majority of Pinterest customers, it represents an additional marketing channel. This addition is expected to produce a near-term margin headwind of around 100 bps for Pinterest during 2026. However, this appears to be priced in, leaving potential upside if integration goes better than expected.

Valuation

At ~$20, PINS is trading at ~12x forward non-GAAP earnings and ~2.7x forward revenue. For a company with a 14% expected revenue growth rate, 41% adjusted EBITDA margin, $1.25 billion in FCF, and an activist investor committing to buy $1 billion of equity, I believe that these multiples do not fully capture the long-term growth potential.

Applying an earnings multiple of 16x (on estimates of $1.73 EPS), which represents a modest premium to reflect ARPU gap optionality, Elliott-driven capital return, and the Q1/H1 headwinds that have already been absorbed into their guidance, results in a stock price of ~$27.68 per share. This is my 12-month price target, which implies ~38% from current levels.

Risks to the Thesis

Q4 2025 revenue was a miss due to advertiser budget cuts, introducing uncertainty that could continue into this year. If budget cuts continue or if consumer spending weakens, it could cause the multiple to compress further.

The sales force restructuring could also cause near-term disruptions. If this impact prolongs or has a larger interruption than management predicts, it could increase execution risk.

Ad-pricing compression is another risk. If international users monetize slower than expected, revenue growth could be below expectations.

Takeaway

Pinterest is evolving from a niche discovery platform into a large-scale commerce platform utilizing AI that has multiple growth opportunities. High-intent user behavior, international monetization opportunities, and measurable advertiser ROI provide a path to compound revenue and margins over time.

The company also provides an attractive opportunity given activist support, aggressive share buybacks, and clear execution on all key strategic initiatives. If the company can continue to close the monetization gap and maintain consistent AI-driven performance improvements, it could trigger a potential re-rating. Pinterest is no longer simply a social media platform but an under-monetized, high-intent advertisement-based ecosystem with numerous potential drivers for creating value for shareholders. This is why I'm rating the stock a Buy with a price target of $27.68 per share.

data.title: Netflix: Advertising Could Become A Real Drag On The Business
data.description:
Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) is, as of this writing, down 10% after hours following the release of its Q1 earnings report. The general consensus has pointed to a variety of factors to explain this; the leading two are the company’s softer-than-expected guidance for Q2 and the departure of co-founder and long-time CEO Reed Hastings, who, three years after vacating the big chair to become chairman of the Board, will now leave the company entirely.

While those factors have their place, I would emphasize the operational indicators in the report above all other considerations. Netflix, I think, is declining because some of its metrics are getting a little disquieting.

Metrics, Not People

I am not convinced Hastings’s departure is a significant factor here. In general, I tend to discount the importance of personalities in corporate operations; while leadership can make a huge difference, leaders do so through their decisions, not their mere presence. Hastings, by all accounts, has allowed co-CEOs Greg Peters and Ted Sarandos to do things their way since stepping back, so I frankly just don’t think this changes very much.

One sign of Hastings' relatively hands-off approach was the merger with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD)—which, yes, I know, did not go through. But that was thanks to Paramount’s (PSKY) frankly ludicrous overbid. Just the fact that Netflix was looking to buy was in many ways a renunciation of Hastings' “build, not buy” mantra that he had preached through 20 years of leading the company.

Merger Windfall

That merger itself was a substantial factor in this earnings report. Remember, Netflix was owed a $2.8 billion termination fee from Warner Brothers—really from Paramount, who has agreed to reimburse Warner for it—that it recorded this quarter in “other income.”

Netflix’s “strong” earnings report owes entirely to this extra payment. Without that payment, Netflix would have reported a $2.9 billion quarterly net income—roughly 68 cents per share, considerably below the 76 cents that the company had projected going in.

This shortfall was uncoupled from Netflix’s revenue growth, which at $12.25 billion actually came in above expectations. Netflix is growing revenue faster than ever, but its profit is not following as some expected it would.

Advertising Struggles

I would attribute this to Netflix’s advertising operation. On the surface, Netflix’s newest product innovation appears to be making strides. At the end of 2023, 30% of Netflix sign-ups were ad-supported; today, management reports that 60% are. Netflix is expected to “roughly double” its ad revenue to $3 billion in 2026. My argument from the beginning, however, has been that Netflix will have a much harder time turning ad revenue into profits than it does subscription revenue.

Netflix announced its new ad tier back in early 2022, as its stock was tanking 75% from its COVID peak to the sub-$200 level where I recommended buying back in after being out for a few years. Since then, ads have gone live, and Netflix stock has soared back up, leading more than a few to draw a direct connection between the two.

They shouldn’t. Ads have never been what was driving the Netflix rebound. In fact, almost from the moment they launched, Netflix ads have had issues. Netflix officially launched ads on November 3rd, 2022, and Netflix itself seemed to think it would have 40 million ad-supported accounts by no later than the end of 2023. Three months into the new year and five months after launch, Netflix had only just crossed 1 million ad-supported users. They finished at 23 million at the end of 2023.

Netflix eventually did get to 40 million ad-supported users in May 2024. This was because management began to take further steps to augment their ad totals, though whether such steps augmented the company as a whole is less clear. Essentially, tired of waiting, Netflix began pushing people onto the ad tier who didn’t want to be there.

One of the biggest boosts to Netflix’s ad-supported count came when T-Mobile’s Netflix On Us feature was downgraded from the ad-free Standard plan to the ad-supported one in early 2024. With T-Mobile representing 1/3 of the US wireless market, this alone probably eliminated most of the target shortfall at the time. T-Mobile management later reported the changes had been “well accepted,” suggesting not many users paid the fee to upgrade back to no ads.

Back To Basics

Many subsequent steps have been in the same vein. Netflix has achieved considerable growth in ad-supported accounts but has done so largely by moving away from the customer-first philosophy that was always the company’s hallmark. The biggest example of all was Basic. Another major obstacle to ads growth was the company actually offered two “bargain” services: Standard with Ads and Basic ad-free.

Netflix management stated that they hadn't seen the switching from ad-free to ads that they expected and found that nearly as many value-conscious shoppers were choosing Basic without Ads as Standard with Ads. That should have been good news; management had misidentified what consumers wanted—they really didn’t want ads—but it was alright because they had a bargain option for them as well.

Management’s response was quite different: they simply eliminated the Basic plan completely in territories where ad-supported was live.

The Price Of Pushing

Again, this worked. Netflix is now believed to have almost 200 million monthly active users on ad-supported plans, which, using my household conversion calculation, would pencil out to around 60 million accounts or so. But these short-term gains can have long-term consequences. One of the biggest downsides of pushing people into ad plans they don’t want is that it can produce “engagement friction,” basically the increased tendency of someone annoyed by ads to reduce the number of hours they watch.

Engagement friction at Netflix is actually getting pretty bad. With the latest engagement report released a few months ago, covering the second half of 2025, Netflix reported 96 billion hours viewed in 2H2025. Netflix disclosed it had hit 325 million subscribers by the end-2025; that’s 1.64 hours per day, per account. It’s actually a little bit more, since the weighted average subscriber base was a bit lower, but as long as we’re consistent, we can just as easily do comparisons this way.

That’s a staggering 25% fall from the peak. When was that peak? The first half of 2023, right before Netflix started shoving everyone onto ad-supported. Netflix reported 93 billion hours viewed, which, spread over almost 100 million fewer accounts, came to 2.17 hours per day.

The Power Of Engagement

I won’t repeat everything I’ve written about engagement and its relation to profit margins. The two are strongly correlated, but not for the reasons some would assume. My most recent piece on the issue with regard to Netflix is here, but engagement is the critical metric of any streamer—it's almost impossible to cover all of its myriad effects in one article.

I think the most important thing to remember is that engagement’s impact is not linear; a company can lose its entire profit from a subscriber even without that subscriber’s hours viewed dropping to zero; that is, even if that subscriber doesn’t leave the service, they can go from profitable to unprofitable for Netflix.

I explained why before, but in a nutshell, when a subscriber watches less and becomes “borderline” for renewal, Netflix loses leverage over content creators and must pay the full demanded rate for whatever last piece of content is keeping that subscriber anchored to the service. At low enough levels of engagement, Netflix becomes little more than a pass-through vehicle for content creators, keeping veritably nothing for itself.

When Netflix subscribers watch multiple content verticals on Netflix, it can analyze each one to see which is cheapest to renew and bargain harder with each creator to reduce costs. If engagement on a given account rises high enough, Netflix can even theoretically achieve a negative content cost for that subscriber—i.e., a gross margin of over 100%.

Engagement friction, on the other hand, reverses this effect. As hours viewed decline, Netflix loses operating leverage and must pay higher rates for content. Netflix’s recent numbers are consistent with this theory. After several years of holding steady at $17 billion, Netflix is continuing to increase subscriber levels…but announced that its content spending would be hiked to $20 billion this year. The damage from engagement friction isn’t the subscribers who leave; it's the higher spending required to prevent them from leaving.

"Core Netflix": Ad-Free Entertainment Still Solid

For all my skepticism about Netflix advertising, however, I still think Netflix has good potential. This is because its core profit engine, subscription-based, ad-free entertainment programming, remains the industry leader. Even now, upwards of 80% of Netflix’s subscribers remain ad-free. While management has not handled the advertising issue as I would have preferred, the subscriber base has not—yet—been severely damaged by it.

I also commend the company, in particular co-CEO Greg Peters, for protecting its industry-leading recommendation algorithms from manipulation. Reports have been prevalent for a while that Sarandos wanted to “adjust” the algorithm to steer more viewership towards Netflix’s own originals and away from library content. Peters insisted on putting the changes through rigorous A/B testing, as Netflix does with most of its innovations.

When, as might be expected, the “Ted Row” proved to be a debacle, Peters killed the idea. This commitment to original, “core Netflix” is what the company will need to recover lost ground.

AI Is Coming Fast

Netflix may also benefit more than others from ongoing technological disruption in the media space. Historically, Netflix has been at the forefront of disruption in media, embracing things like recommendation algorithms, binge-watching, and indeed even the idea of streaming itself long before traditional media operators. The hope is that it will continue to do so. Right now, a movie called Bitcoin: Killing Satoshi, starring Gal Gadot and Casey Affleck, is putting the finishing touches on a film that is widely billed as the first AI-integrated production from a major studio.

To be clear, Gadot and Affleck haven’t been replaced by computer images—the acting is quite real. But a lot of the supporting crew functions that the audience doesn’t see in front of the camera—lighting, location scouting, sound dubbing, etc.—have been supplanted by AI generation on a special soundstage where the movie was shot. Early estimates are that the film, which has a $70 million budget, would have cost $300 million using a traditional approach.

If Netflix can embrace AI and achieve such massive cost savings faster than its competitors, it will have a competitive advantage in scripted content considerably stronger than the one it already enjoys.

Investment Summary

I bought Netflix at a split-adjusted $20 and sold out at $72. It’s true I missed out on some upside, but make no mistake, it wasn’t ad-driven upside. The single biggest driver of Netflix, like most of the market since I sold out, has actually been politics. In fact, if you measure Netflix from the end of December 2024 instead of early November, when I sold, its entire outperformance over the S&P 500 (SP500) since that time disappears. And that was before the earnings report; when Netflix opens in a few hours, it will be $10 cheaper and substantially below the growth rate of the S&P since the end-2024.

I do not regret selling when I did, even knowing what I know about how far from the peak we were in November 2024. I remain convinced that the advertising shift in company strategy is not working as well as commonly believed; the engagement friction issue, I think, has real implications for the company. However, the issues in ads/sports/live should be weighed against the fact that its ad-free entertainment business remains strong.

For now, I rank Netflix a Hold and will not go long or short.

data.title: Vertiv Q1 Earnings Preview - Why This AI Enabler Remains A Buy
data.description:
I Remain Bullish On VRT Ahead Of Earnings

Since my upgrade on Vertiv Holdings (VRT) in late January last year, the stock has managed to double, strongly outperforming most benchmarks (even those focused on the tech sector specifically), as the firm's fundamentals kept improving at a faster-than-usual pace due to an AI demand spike.

The catalysts I saw in my previous articles have largely played out, and VRT has started to look a bit too stretched and valuation-extended, so to speak. Nevertheless, I believe there's still some upside ahead as the organic order inflows keep coming in, and VRT's core solutions—thermal and power management systems that allow data centers to function without melting down or blacking out—gain more and more attention from hyperscalers. Assuming VRT beats the consensus throughout 2026 as it was doing throughout 2025 (probably at a more modest pace), I think the stock stops looking too expensive. I expect some volatility coming in when Vertiv reports its upcoming Q1 2026 results (next week, 04/22/2026), but I generally view the odds for a beat to be quite high, and I like VRT for the medium term, even if it might correct for a while. Based on this, I still rate VRT as a "Buy".

Why Do I Think So?

In its last reporting quarter, VRT showed $2.88 billion in sales (+23% YoY) with an adjusted EPS of $1.36 (+37% YoY), beating the consensus estimates only on the bottom line by 4.90%. Despite the slight miss on the top line (less than 1%, per Seeking Alpha), the stock rose by over 20% on the earnings report and has since managed to add another 20%. What drove up the sentiment was the organic order flow—in Q4 alone, VRT saw its order book rise by 252% YoY and 117% QoQ, so now the backlog amounts to $15 billion (it more than doubled YoY). Vertiv now is a $115 billion market cap firm (following the recent rally), so it might seem that the above backlog looks good but not extraordinary. But the growth story ahead isn't just about the orders.

We know that hyperscalers' needs for power densities are rising as never before, versus the historical average of 5-10 kW consumption to upward of 100-120 kW for AI workloads. In this environment, liquid cooling and advanced power orchestration become mandatory parts of any capacity addition. And Vertiv is actively investing to meet the rising demand by expanding its manufacturing presence in Ohio (they want to bring in a 45% increase in production capacity for liquid cooling and chilled water systems by Q2 2027) and in Malaysia (should be online since early 2026). They're also getting ready to release an 800V DC power portfolio in 2H 2026 to support the 2027 rollout of Nvidia's (NVDA) Rubin Ultra platforms, which is very likely to capture higher dollar-per-megawatt content in new data center builds going forward.

...we see fabrication, and we like to talk about this integrated and converged infrastructure... we see that, that helps us add more value and capture more value. So it's something that drives towards an increase in megawatt -- sorry, dollars per megawatt.

Source: VRT's CEO at JPM's conference

According to Grand View Research's study, the DC cooling market is set to expand at a CAGR of 22.3% up to 2033, and Vertiv's decision to transition from just selling hardware to end-to-end lifecycle services for these complex thermal systems is telling me that the firm has set itself up for a structurally higher margin ahead. The management noted that they're targeting 25% in adjusted EBIT margin by 2029:

Our year-over-year incremental margin in the third quarter was approximately 30%, a good indication that we continue the path towards full year adjusted operating margin target of 25% in 2029.

Source: Seeking Alpha Earnings Insights Transcript, VRT's Q3 2025 print

Compared to 2025, it's going to be a massive margin expansion of 460 bps. For reference, the change in margin from 2024 to 2025 was about 100 bps, as far as I can see, and the operating leverage from this was already stunning. And the business expansion, thanks to the above projected CAGR, isn't going anywhere as well. I believe the scaling plan through 2027-2028 will give Vertiv a chance to capture maximum value as AI racks push past 100 kW.

Now, let's take a look at what's currently expected from Vertiv with respect to its upcoming Q1 release.

The management guided for Q1 2026 sales of $2.5-2.7 billion, which is clearly below the Q4 figure, but it's due to seasonality. The organic growth is going to be between 18% and 26% anywhere, depending on where exactly VRT lands within that range.

The market's consensus tilts toward the guidance's higher end, expecting $2.65 billion in Q1, and while it's above the midpoint, we don't see the same kind of premium we saw in previous quarters. It's likely because in Q4, VRT missed on the top line, so analysts have taken notes for Q1. Be that as it may, let's take a look at the bottom line—the management guided for $0.95 and $1.01 for adjusted quarterly EPS, implying a 53% YoY increase at the midpoint. The sell-side consensus perfectly captures the high end here (still no premium added):

Given the track record of Vertiv's beating in the past, and considering the fact that the consensus doesn't put too much focus on the top line this time after the one-off miss in Q4, I think the firm has great odds for beating the current Q1 consensus expectations.

What was driving the post-earnings narrative last time—the backlog data—won't be in focus this time, in my opinion, because the management decided to stop providing quarterly order updates (we'll see the updates on that front from the 10-Ks going forward). I think that was a correct move—they basically showed that the rise in backlogs is structural, and investors don't need to focus on it on a quarterly basis as it's just noise. What's going to replace backlog updates as the narrative driver this time, in my opinion, are three things:

  1. Backlog conversion—how much of the past backlog has been converted to actual sales during Q1, and were there any severe supply chain bottlenecks?
  2. Margin expansion—was the firm able to hold the guided 18.5-19.5% adj. EBIT range for Q1 amid the impact from tariffs?
  3. Guidance updates—will VRT be able to raise the full-year FY2026 sales/EPS guidance this time?

I don't have the ultimate answers for the above questions, but I assume that nothing fundamentally changed in terms of VRT's prospects for the past quarter. If so, the backlog conversion should be solid, and the execution on margin stabilization should be good as well. And I hope the management will upgrade their previous guidance figures this time (at least for sales).

When it comes to VRT's valuation setup, I understand some investors who consider Vertiv as a very richly priced pick in the niche, as most of the forward metrics are much higher than the sector's medians, according to Seeking Alpha:

However, we see that the forward PEG ratio is green in the table above, meaning that it's below the median figure in the sector.

Zooming out 2-3 years, we see that VRT isn't priced at an unreachable valuation at all—for an FY2028 EPS increase of almost 25%, the stock is valued at an FY2028 P/E of less than 30x, which seems quite reasonable to me.

We see that the consensus expects the YoY expansion to slow down quite sharply by 2028, but we also know that the adjusted EBIT margin that the management sees for 2029 should be 400+ bps higher than we saw in 2025. It tells me that the operating leverage isn't going anywhere. Assuming that VRT beats the FY2027 EPS consensus by 5% and then beats it again by 5% in FY2028—or subsequent bullish revisions come first—the stock's FY2028 P/E multiple falls to:

FY2028 EPS = $6.16 * 1.4655 * 1.05 * 1.2467 * 1.05 = $12.41

FY2028 P/E = $294.13 / $12.41 = 23.7x

Assuming that VRT keeps its 28-30x forward earnings multiple in the next 12-18 months, the upside potential from here might be close to 22-23%. Based on that, I think VRT should still be rated as a "Buy".

Where Can I Be Wrong?

There's a risk that the massive $15 billion backlog Vertiv showed last time has actually been artificially inflated by supply chain panic, as some VRT's customers decided to double-order or place massive advanced orders simply to secure their place in line amid today's rising lead times for most DC components. If it's true, Vertiv's revenue recognition will likely be pushed to the right, meaning that we might see another top-line miss in Q1/Q2 and maybe cuts to the current guidance ahead.

The DC cooling market's prospects look great for VRT, but we can't rule out the competition risks. For example, Eaton (ETN) recently acquired Boyd Thermal Management for $9.5 billion, hence threatening VRT's long-term moat in the coolant distribution unit (CDU) market (VRT holds ~70% of the market there).

There's also a fair concern when it comes to VRT's valuation. I assume a quite high multiple for 2028, and if VRT trades at 20-25x and has no premium on top of its FY2028 EPS consensus, the stock's PT would drop to $227.925/share (-22.51% downside). Furthermore, Wall Street's consensus PT says VRT has run out of its upside potential:

The Bottom Line

Despite the above risk factors, VRT still looks interesting to me in the long term. I agree that after the massive bullish rally of the past weeks, the upside potential has gotten smaller in the short term. But I like the prospects in general and the fundamentals that drive the business. For the upcoming Q1 release, I'm waiting for some updates on the backlog conversion, as well as the fresh margins move (how the negatives from tariffs have been managed). In the next 12-18 months, I believe VRT can expand by another 22-23% if my valuation assumptions are right. Based on that, I'm rating VRT as a "Buy".

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Daikin Industries: Eyes On Re-Rating Potential After Activist Investor Involvement
data.description:
I've assigned a 'Buy' rating to Daikin Industries, Ltd. (DKILY) (DKILF) (6367.T).

An April 16 SA News piece noted that "activist investor" Elliott has taken "a sizable (3%) stake" to engage "the company to boost performance."

In my opinion, DKILY has a good chance of commanding a richer multiple, assuming it adopts the necessary value-unlocking measures.

Corporate Overview

DKILY is among the "largest HVAC companies in the world" according to one of its supplier's disclosures.

Core Business Snapshot

The above-mentioned A/C division contributed 92% of its turnover for the fiscal year ended March 2025 (FY24 as defined by the group). The balance came from chemicals (5%), oil hydraulics (2%), and defense systems (1%).

It generated 42%, 35%, and 16% of FY2024 sales from APAC, the U.S., and Europe, respectively. There's another 7% relating to other geographical regions.

The Market Is Underpricing Its Shares

DKILY is now trading at 22 times FY26 earnings multiple as per S&P Capital IQ. That represents a meaningful 19% discount to its five-year mean of 27x.

Furthermore, the firm's listed comparables, Trane Technologies (TT) and Johnson Controls (JCI), are commanding more demanding forward P/Es of 31x and 28x, respectively.

I estimate the stock's upside to be +34% (29.5/22.0) if its valuations can converge with the peer average (29.5x). In the subsequent sections, I identify possible ways to narrow this gap.

Diverse Profitability Enhancement Levers

My bet is on the group's margins trending upwards in the years ahead.

At its early-Feb 3QFY25 call (S&P Capital IQ transcript), management outlined the aim of "increasing the weight of the solutions business." The firm describes this unit as being involved in asset-light "maintenance, and repair services."

I can imagine its companywide profits improving with the ongoing pivot away from low-return equipment sales.

Separately, DKILY has the intent to enlarge its presence in the "Data Center/DC" end market.

The goal is to triple DC cooling unit revenues to ¥0.3T in FY2030 by scaling the product suite. It's encouraging for me to know that the targeted EBIT-to-sales for DC-related operations is 15%. That's much better than the group-level figure in the high-single-digit range.

Recently Launched DC-Related Offerings

Furthermore, DKILY is becoming leaner.

The company delivered ¥53B of expense cuts in the prior fiscal year. As disclosed in the integrated report, it plans to "reduce operational workload by 40% in FY2025" with the use of "robotic process automation and AI."

The trailing twelve months' EBIT-to-revenue ratios for TT, JCI, and DKILY are 18.2%, 13.8%, and 8.1%, respectively. This sends me a clear message about Daikin's potential to be more profitable like its peers.

A Focused Portfolio Is Desirable

Earlier, I drew attention to DKILY's market leadership in HVAC and its multi-business structure. It's reasonable to assume that the stock will fetch a higher valuation if it were an air-con pure-play.

My take is that the disposal of non-A/C divisions will be value-enhancing. The conglomerate discount is likely to shrink with the holding company owning fewer unrelated entities. Furthermore, oil hydraulics and defense systems are low-margin segments boasting FY24 EBIT-to-topline of 2% and 6%, respectively.

As another reference, UBS Group (UBS) published a report (not publicly available) titled "Medium-Term Plan A Catalyst" on Feb. 27, 2026. The European bank's analysis suggests that DKILY has around ¥0.16T ($1B) of "non-core assets" on its books. That's an indication of the possible asset monetization proceeds it could realize in the future.

Investors Should Reward Improved Shareholder-Friendliness

You would most probably be uninterested in DKILY as a yield-focused investor now. The shares offer a meager trailing dividend-to-price of 1.4%. The last time it purchased any of its stock was over a decade ago in '15.

Japanese media "Nikkei Asia" reported that Elliott has urged DKILY to "buy back up to 1 trillion yen ($6.3 billion) of its shares over the next few years" based on its sources. I think this is fairly realistic, taking into account its strong financial profile.

The firm had ¥985B of cash and short-term investments on its balance sheets as of the end of 2025. It has been "Free Cash Flow/FCF" positive in 18 of the last 20 years. Its FCF for FY2023/2024 averaged around ¥0.2T. These numbers were sourced from S&P Capital IQ.

My view is that DKILY could easily broaden its stockholder base by lifting cash payout or resuming buybacks.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

Elliott might exit its position in the company if the management doesn't take relevant actions to bolster shareholder value. That scenario will also send negative signals about DKILY's receptiveness towards investor engagement.

Demand for the group's air conditioners may drop substantially in the case of a global recession driven by the Middle Eastern conflict. These are products linked to the cyclical residential property markets.

My Concluding Thoughts

I'm a bull on this name.

DKILY's shares are attractively priced compared to history and peers. I see the recent entry of an activist as a critical catalyst to re-rate the stock.

data.title: Chimera Investment Corporation Notes Analysis: CIMP Gets The Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

A previous article analyzed the four preferred stocks issued by Chimera Investment Corporation (CIM). Here, I move up the capital structure to analyze the three Notes available to investors, those being:

  • Chimera Investment Corp 9.000% Senior Notes due 5/15/2029, Chimera Investment Corporation CAL 29 (CIMN).
  • Chimera Investment Corp 9.250% Senior Notes due 08/15/2029, Chimera Investment Corporation NT 29 (CIMO).
  • Chimera Investment Corp 8.875% Senior Notes due 8/15/2030, Chimera Investment Corporation NT 30 (CIMP).

Based on its better YTC, YTM, last Maturity date, longest built-in call protection, and least likely to get Called, my choice, thus the Buy rating, goes to CIMP.

Chimera Investment Corporation Review

The best due diligence starting point for Notes is with the issuer, as one needs to gauge if there is interest and/or redemption risk in owning these assets.

Seeking Alpha describes this mREIT as (edited):

Chimera Investment Corporation operates as a real estate investment trust in the United States. The company, through its subsidiaries, invests in a portfolio of mortgage assets, including residential mortgage loans, non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities, agency mortgage-backed securities secured, agency mortgage-backed securities secured by pools of commercial mortgage loans, business purpose and investor loans, and other real estate-related securities.

Source: Seeking Alpha CIM.

Chimera describes themselves as:

We are a credit-focused hybrid mortgage REIT, meaning that our portfolio contains residential mortgage loans and both Agency and non-Agency mortgage-backed securities. Our hybrid approach allows us to maintain flexibility in portfolio allocation and liability management. Through the use of securitization and leverage, we aim to create long-term value for our shareholders.

We invest in RMBS issued by third parties or issued or guaranteed by Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae, or Freddie Mac (Agency RMBS). Our Agency CMBS assets are primarily Ginnie Mae Construction Loan and Ginnie Mae Permanent Loan Certificates with prepayment protection.

Source: Chimera REIT.

The latest investor presentation highlighted the following, starting with their approach to the mortgage market.

The next diagram illustrates their strategy for building long-term shareholder value.

The next two tables provide details on their holdings portfolio.

The last line above shows the delinquency rate; the next shows how those rates have been changing since late 2022.

Now I will turn to the financial statements to get a gauge on the safety of the interest payments and the par value of the Notes. First is the income statement, where I compare net income to Notes interest owed.

With my calculations showing CIM owes just over $22MM in interest on their Notes, coverage is just over 10X, a solid coverage ratio.

Next, I look at the balance sheet to see how much equity is available to pay off the par value of the Notes, which I calculated at $245MM.

Again, the ratio is just over 10X; thus, I would rate defaulting as very low.

Comparing the Notes

Too bad I didn’t submit this article at the start of April, as Note prices are up sharply since then. Looking for a reason, I found an article about Tudor Investment Corporation raising its share of the outstanding common by 35% to about a 0.4% stake. The article also noted there has been insider buying recently.

The following table compares the key features across the three Notes and the Chimera Investment Corp. 8.00% Series B Fixed/Float Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock, Chimera Investment Corporation PFD SER B (CIM.PR.B), which earned a Buy rating in my last article for comparing Notes to the best Preferred stock.

Factor CIMN CIMO CIMP PFD D
Issued 5/15/24 8/12/24 9/9/25 2/22/17
Size (MM shares) 2.6 2.6 4.6 12.0
Coupon 9.00% 9.25% 8.875% 9.75%*
Price $25.50 $25.68 $25.44 $24.14
Yield 8.8% 9.1% 8.9% 10.2%
Call date 5/15/26 8/15/26 8/15/27 3/30/24
YTC NA** .8% 7.5% NA
Maturity date 5/15/29 8/15/29 8/15/30 NA
YTM 8.3% 8.3% 8.4% NA

* Floating formula: 3-month SOFR + 6.052%.

** I chose not to provide YTC as my calculator isn’t precise enough for such a short time remaining.

Both CIMO and CIMP were rated as BBB by Egan-Jones Ratings when issued, which seems to be the only rating they ever assign. I did not see in any Prospectus that Chimera was allowed to delay interest payments as some Notes allow. With the 5-year UST rate being very volatile since CIMN was issued, it is hard to tell if the markets changed their demand for what it costs CIM to issue each Note.

Analysis

  • All Note coupons are fixed, whereas the preferred has a very attractive floating formula.
  • As investors might expect, PFD-D has a better yield, given that it falls below the Notes in the capital structure.
  • Even accounting for its pending interest payment, CIMN would have a negative YTC.
  • CIMO, at current prices, has a slightly higher yield.
  • CIMP has the best YTC and YTM values, and its lowest coupon should make it the last Note Called once all three are eligible.

Conclusion

Based on its better YTC, YTM, last Maturity date, longest built-in Call protection, plus the least likely to get Called, my choice, thus the Buy rating, goes to CIMP. That said, the yield on the preferred makes it an attractive choice.

Portfolio strategy

If an investor wants to bypass funds for preferred stocks and select and weigh issues individually, here are the factors that I think should come into their due diligence process.

  • Coupon type: Fixed rates provide exact knowledge of what the dividend will be, whereas floating rates will move when market rates move.
  • Payment risk: As covered here, is the yield provided at purchase enough to compensate for the potential risk of non-payment.
  • Floating formula: The higher the fixed add-on rate, the better (mostly). I like ones using the 5-year UST rate versus the 3-month SOFR, though the shorter SOFR has benefits too. One caveat: a generous floating formula increases the chance of being Called.
  • Call date: A common feature is five years of Call protection. That said, many have covenants that allow for early Calls.
  • Maturity date: Unlike most preferred stocks, almost all Notes have a maturity date. The longer that is, the greater the odds the coupon floats.
  • Selection: By this I’m referring to, as is the case with Chimera, which coupon is best to own? By not owning the highest coupon, the investor should gain extra Call protection.

The interplay of these factors and where interest rates are in the market will help determine what the yield, YTC, and any YTM investors can collect by buying the issue.
